title: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
author: Tim Gillespie
orientation: Tim Gillespie answers a pro-life pastor's claim that obeying Roe v. Wade is required by Romans 13, arguing that the chapter's full context exposes Roe as evil rather than legitimate authority and that, under America's constitutional republic, 'we the people' are the governing authority Romans 13 has in view.
audiences: Pro-life Christian who cites Romans 13 to oppose immediate abolition
Pastor weighing whether to preach defiance of Roe v. Wade
Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
Abolitionist looking for biblical and civic arguments for nullification
Skeptic who thinks abolitionists are lawless or anti-government

questions.question: Does Romans 13 actually require Christians to comply with Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who cites Romans 13 to oppose immediate abolition
questions.answer: No. Gillespie argues pro-lifers truncate the passage at verses 1-2 and ignore that Romans 13 describes rulers who praise good and avenge evil. A regime that protects the slaughter of image-bearers fits none of those descriptions, so invoking Romans 13 to demand compliance with Roe inverts the text.
questions.quote: The problem is pro-lifers usually either stop right there or at the end of verse 2.

questions.question: Why is calling Roe 'the law of the land' a category error?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Because legislatures make laws while courts only issue opinions. Roe is a Supreme Court opinion, not a statute, and nothing in the Constitution authorizes the Court to compel states to permit abortion. Treating an unconstitutional opinion as binding law confuses the actual structure of American government.
questions.quote: Legislatures make laws.  Courts hand down opinions.  Roe is NOT the law of the land; it is a court’s opinion – and it is wrong.

questions.question: Who is the 'governing authority' Romans 13 has in view in the American context?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing whether to preach defiance of Roe v. Wade
questions.answer: Gillespie contends that in a constitutional republic the people themselves are the governing authority. Presidents, governors, legislators, and judges are hired employees bound by the Constitution, not crowned rulers like Caesar or Herod, so applying Romans 13 to them as if they were sovereign monarchs misreads both the text and our form of government.
questions.quote: The president, governor, legislators and judges of America and the states are hired, not crowned.  We are the governing authority.

questions.question: Is there historical Christian precedent for defying unjust court decrees?
questions.audience: Skeptic who thinks abolitionists are lawless or anti-government
questions.answer: Yes. Gillespie points to Christians who resisted the Dred Scott decision and the Fugitive Slave Act, recognizing that pro-slavery rulings from black-robed judges were evil and that conscience required defiance. The same principle applies to Roe.
questions.quote: This is the same reason Christians stood up to black-robed oligarchs over the Dred Scott decision and the Fugitive Slave Act.  They knew slavery and pro-slavery decrees were evil and their [Christian conscience dictated they defy evil]

questions.question: Why does Gillespie say incrementalism cannot accomplish its own stated goal?
questions.audience: Abolitionist looking for biblical and civic arguments for nullification
questions.answer: Because SCOTUS will never voluntarily revisit Roe; only a state defying the opinion can force the issue back before the Court. Incremental regulations leave Roe untouched, so the strategy is logically incapable of producing the overturn its advocates claim to want.
questions.quote: Incrementalism will never cause Roe to be overturned.  It will only be overturned when a state stands up to the oligarchs and defies Roe, forcing SCOTUS to revisit the issue.

questions.question: Even if Roe were upheld after defiance, would defiance still be justified?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing whether to preach defiance of Roe v. Wade
questions.answer: Yes. Abolitionism is not contingent on a favorable court ruling. Because abortion is the murder of image-bearers, refusing to enforce or obey laws that authorize it is the only righteous response regardless of how the Court rules.
questions.quote: That said, that isn’t really even the point of abolitionism because if the court were to uphold Roe, defiance would still be the only right and Godly reaction.

questions.question: What does Gillespie say is at stake when Christians stay complacent under Romans 13 rhetoric?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who cites Romans 13 to oppose immediate abolition
questions.answer: He warns that motives may vary, but the outcome of accepting the pro-life movement's reading of Romans 13 is uniform: the ongoing killing of unborn children. Conscience cannot be soothed by passivity in the face of mass slaughter sanctioned by an oligarchic court.
questions.quote: But the result is the same: the continued slaughter of the unborn image-bearer of God on the altar of convenience and hedonism.

related.title: WATCH: Romans 13 Teaches That Civil Government Must Submit to Christ
related.reason: Develops the same Romans 13 hermeneutic in video form, arguing civil rulers are themselves under Christ's authority rather than autonomous.

related.title: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
related.reason: Provides a biblical case study of a godly ruler immediately abolishing evil rather than incrementally regulating it, complementing Gillespie's call to defy Roe.

related.title: Defiance of Tyrants Must Be the New Republican Litmus Test
related.reason: Extends Gillespie's nullification argument into a political litmus test for officeholders who claim to oppose abortion.

related.title: Christian, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the same conscience/obedience question Gillespie raises by distinguishing Christian abolition from pro-life pragmatism.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Reinforces Gillespie's critique that incrementalism cannot accomplish its stated end goal and corrodes Christian witness.

related.title: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Echoes Gillespie's appeal to historic Christians who defied unjust authorities, urging similar courage from today's pastors.
